coredata.dc:title: Novel nitrogen doped carbon dots for corrosion inhibition of carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Mingjun
Ren, Siming
Zhao, Haichao
Wang, Liping
Xue, Qunji
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized and used for inhibiting the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in hydrochloric acid (HCl) solution. Inhibition effectiveness in short- and long-term immersion was examined using electrochemical measurements, weight loss and surface analysis. Results revealed the inhibition efficiency was improved significantly after adding NCDs and strongly dependent on the concentration of NCDs. SVET results showed that the sample immersed in HCl solution with NCDs showed lower anodic current density mapping than that in blank HCl solution. According to the data extracted from the Langmuir adsorption, the absorption of NCDs involved both chemisorption and physisorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Q235 carbon steel
NCDs
Electrochemical measurements
SVET method
Corrosion inhibition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The characterization of p-CDs and o-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by the Open Financial Grant from Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology ( QNLM2016ORP0409 ), the Key Research Projects of Frontier Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences ( QYZDY-SSW-JSC009 ) and “One Hundred Talented People” of the Chinese Academy of Sciences ( Y60707WR04 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of the p-CDs (a) and o-CDs (b) by the solvothermal reaction of p-PD and o-PD, and the photographs of CDs dispersed in ethanol (left) and 1 M HCl solution (right) with the concentration of 100 mg/mL.
SPM images and TEM insets of p-CDs (a), o-CDs (b) and the height-profiles along the corresponding lines in Fig. 2a and b.
XPS survey scans of p-PD and p-CDs (a) as well as o-PD and o-CDs (b).
XPS spectra of p-CDs (a–c) and o-CDs (d–f) for C 1s, N 1s and O 1s.
Raman spectra of p-PD and p-CDs (a) as well as o-PD and o-CDs (b).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of p-PD and p-CDs, (b) UV-vis absorption spectra of o-PD and o-CDs.
Potentiodynamic polarization curves of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of p-CDs (a) and o-CDs (b) at the room temperature.
The current density profiles of carbon steel after 2 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution with the absence (a) and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs (b) and o-CDs (c) at the room temperature.
Nyquist and bode plots of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of p-CDs (a–c) and o-CDs (d–f) at the room temperature.
The equivalent electrical circuits to fit the EIS results in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of 10 mg/L o-CDs (a), and in other cases (b).

The variation of the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency in the presence of various concentrations of p-CDs (a) and o-CDs (c), as well as the Langmuir plots for p-CDs (b) and o-CDs (d) on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution.
Variation of the corrosion rate (a) and inhibition efficiency (b) in the absence and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs after various immersion times.
The surface roughness (Ra ) of the polished Q235 carbon steel before (a) and after 72 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution in the absence (b) and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs (c) and o-CDs (d).
The SEM images for the polished Q235 carbon steel before (a) and after 72 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution in the absence (b) and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs (c) and o-CDs (d).
The EDS spectra of the Q235 carbon steel before (a) and after 72 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution in the absence (b) and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs (c) and o-CDs (d).
The relative contents of C, N and O atoms for p-PD, p-CDs, o-PD and o-CDs.

Electrochemical parameters obtained from potentiodynamic polarization curves of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs at room temperature.
Fitting parameters obtained from EIS results of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs at the room temperature.
Percentage weight contents of elements obtained from EDS spectra.

head.ce:title: Novel nitrogen doped carbon dots for corrosion inhibition of carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies
Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized and used for inhibiting the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in hydrochloric acid (HCl) solution. Inhibition effectiveness in short- and long-term immersion was examined using electrochemical measurements, weight loss and surface analysis. Results revealed the inhibition efficiency was improved significantly after adding NCDs and strongly dependent on the concentration of NCDs. SVET results showed that the sample immersed in HCl solution with NCDs showed lower anodic current density mapping than that in blank HCl solution. According to the data extracted from the Langmuir adsorption, the absorption of NCDs involved both chemisorption and physisorption.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic inhibitors have been employed to inhibit the corrosion of industrial metals and alloys for acid pickling, cleaning, descaling and oil-well acidizing. Generally, the organic inhibitors containing heteroatoms (such as nitrogen, sulfur, oxygen and phosphorus), aromatic rings and π bonds can adsorb on the metal surface via formation of a coordinate covalent bond (chemical adsorption) or the electrostatic interaction between the metal and inhibitor (physical adsorption), thus inhibiting the corrosion of the metals [1–4]. A number of organic compounds have been investigated and employed as corrosion inhibitors to prevent the corrosion of the metals. However, many conventional corrosion inhibitors are being prevented owing to the increasing awareness of human health and ecological pollution associated with traditional inhibitors. Thus, attempts have been made with the hope of developing highly efficient, safe and non-toxic corrosion inhibitors.

Carbon dots (CDs) with high water solubility, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and unique photoluminescence (PL) properties, have attracted ever-increasing attention [5–8]. The major investigations on the CDs focus on the multicolor cellular imaging capability with a single excitation wavelength. However, in recent report, Zhu and coworkers [9] first reported that CDs incorporated polymers not only possessed self-healing properties but also had superior anticorrosion properties, as a comparison with those of the pure polymer coatings, which were attributed to the bonding between CDs, CDs-polymer chain bonding and the four-electron path ORR capability that could reduce oxygen to water. Subsequently, Cui et al. also prepared N doped carbon dots (NCDs) with aminosalicylic acid (ASA) as precursor and found that these NCDs could effectively inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution owing to its special structure [10]. However, there was still rare investigation on this novel and eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor. Therefore, in the present work, the corrosion inhibition ability of NCDs obtained from p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution were studied using potentiodynamic polarization curves, scanning vibrating electrode technique (SVET), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), weight loss measurement and scanning electron microscope (SEM).

p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) were purchased from Aladdin. Ethanol and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 39%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Q235 carbon steel substrates (3.0 cm × 2.0 cm × 1.5 mm) were purchased from local suppliers. Deionized water (DI) was used during whole experiments.

The synthesis of the p-CDs and o-CDs followed a similar procedure reported by Lin et al. [7]. p-PD or o-PD (0.60 g) was first ultrasonically dissolved in ethanol (60 mL), and then the mixtures were transferred into poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclaves. After heating at 180 °C in oven for 12 h and cooling down to room temperature naturally, dark-red and orange suspensions were obtained from p-PD and o-PD. The crude product was purified with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off ∼3.0 kDa) for 1 day to remove the nonreactive molecules. After removal of solvents and further drying under vacuum, p-CDs and o-CDs were obtained.

The Q235 carbon steel substrates (2.0 cm × 3.0 cm × 1.5 mm) were abraded with SiC abrasive papers of 300, 800 and 1000 grit, respectively. Then they were cleaned successively with acetone and alcohol, and finally dried with nitrogen gas. The 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of various concentrations (10, 50, 100 and 200 mg L−1) of p-CDs or o-CDs was prepared from concentrated hydrochloric acid.

Scanning probe microscope (SPM, Veeco Dimension 3100 V) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F20) were performed to examine the morphology and size of the prepared p-CDs and o-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA DLD) was employed to examine the chemical composition and bonding status. The Raman spectrum was recorded with a Renishaw inVia Reflex excited by 532 nm laser. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Lambda 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted in a CHI660E electrochemical station with a conventional three-electrode system. A platinum foil and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as counter and reference electrode, respectively. The Q235 carbon steel with the exposure area of 1 cm2 was the working electrode. All tests were conducted at room temperature. Before the test, open circuit potential test (OCP) was carried out for 30 min to reach a steady state. Then EIS was performed in a frequency range of 100 kHz–10 mHz at the OCP with 10 mV of disturbance signal. The EIS data were fitted and analyzed with Zsimpwin 3.21 software. Eventually, potentiodynamic polarization tests were conducted in the potential range of ±500 mV versus the OCP with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The same test was performed for 3 times to ensure reliability.

The current density mapping at the Q235 carbon steel surface during the immersion in corrosive medium was measured by the VersaScan SVET module from Princenton Applied Research (USA) using a Pt/Ir probe with a 10 μm of diameter. The amplitude of vibration was about 30 μm, the vibration frequency was 80 Hz along the Z direction and the scanned area was 1.0 mm × 1.0 mm. Before the test, the Q235 carbon steel was immersed in the corrosive medium for 2 h.

Weight loss tests were carried out in a 20 mL vessel placed in a constant temperature thermostat. Samples with a dimension of 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 1.5 mm were immersed in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of various concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs. The samples were removed, washed with DI, dried and weighed at various time intervals. The average value of weight loss was used for calculations.

In order to measure the surface roughness of Q235 carbon steel sample after 72 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs, laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, LSM700) was employed. The morphology and surface composition of Q235 carbon steel after 72 h immersion in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of 200 mg L−1 of p-CDs and o-CDs were examined with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of p-CDs and o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of p-CDs and o-CDs are achieved by heating the p-PD and o-PD in ethanol at 180 °C for 12 h. During the heating process, CDs were synthesized by the polymerization of precursor (condensation and intra-molecular cyclization), leading to the formation of CDs containing amino/hydroxyl functional groups on their surfaces. The corresponding structures are proposed according to previous reports [11–14]. The resulting products can be homogeneously dispersed into various solvents. As shown in Fig. 1 , crimson and yellow solutions (from p-CDs and o-CDs, respectively) are observed when they are dispersed in ethanol. Red and orange solutions (from p-CDs and o-CDs, respectively) are observed when they are dispersed in 1 M HCl solution.

Subsequently, SPM and TEM are performed to examine the morphologies of p-CDs and o-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b, the average sizes are about 15–20 nm for p-CDs and 4–6 nm for o-CDs, respectively, which are consistent with the TEM insets in Fig. 2. Most of the CDs are monodispersed while there are also some aggregated CDs. The variations of chemical compositions and bonds of the samples are checked with XPS spectra. The full XPS spectra of p-PD, p-CDs, o-PD and o-CDs as well as the corresponding contents of the elements are shown in Fig. 3 and Table 1 . It can be found that they have same elemental compositions (i.e. C, N and O). However, the content of O and N shows a distinct variation after solvothermal reaction, owing to the occurrence of the decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions during the formation of CDs.

Further, a detailed analysis on the XPS spectra of p-CDs and o-CDs is performed. It can be found from Fig. 4 that p-CDs and o-CDs exhibit similar C 1s, N 1s and O 1s spectra. The C1s spectra of p-CDs and o-CDs (Fig. 4a and d) display two peaks at 284.2 and 285.2 eV, which are attributed to CC and CN, respectively [7]. The N1s spectra are shown in Fig. 4b and e where the two component peaks are assigned to pyridinic N (398.8 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.4 eV) [6,7,15]. For O 1s spectra (Fig. 4c and 4f), the peak at binding energy of 531.8 eV is assigned to COH/COC.

The Raman spectra of p-PD, p-CDs, o-PD and o-CDs are shown Fig. 5 . For the p-PD, the peaks at 1612 cm−1, 1264 cm−1, 1174 cm−1, 843 cm−1 and 647 cm−1 are assigned to benzene ring stretching deformation, CN stretching, CH in plane bending, the benzene ring breathing and bending deformation, respectively [16]. In case of the o-PD, the peaks at 1592 cm−1, 1337 cm−1, 1275 cm−1, 1033 cm−1 and 762 cm−1 correspond to NH2 scissoring mode, CH in plane bending, CN stretching, the benzene ring deformation and breathing, respectively [16]. After the solvothermal reaction, the p-CDs and o-CDs exhibit two featured bands. The peak located at 1397 cm−1 is assigned to the CN+ stretching of the delocalized polaronic charge carriers [17,18]. The band between 1500 and 1600 cm−1 corresponds to the N-H bending deformation mode and the CC deformation of benzenoid rings and quinoid rings.

Fig. 6 a shows UV–vis spectra of p-PD and p-CDs. The peaks observed at 243 and 308 nm are the characteristic peaks of p-PD. After 12 h of solvothermal reaction, a new peak appears at 454 nm for p-CDs. Similarly, there are also two characteristic peaks at 239 and 295 nm for o-PD and o-CDs, and a new absorption peak occurs at 428 nm for o-CDs. These two new peaks at 454 nm and 428 nm are assigned to the excitonic transition in quinoid-imine units of long conjugated polymer chain [7,19].

Polarization technique is available to monitor the progress and mechanism of the anodic and cathodic reactions as well as evaluate the effect of inhibitor concentration on either cathodic or anodic reactions [20,21]. Potentiodynamic polarization curves of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution with various concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs are given in Fig. 7 . The values of corrosion current density (icorr ), corrosion potential (Ecorr ), cathodic and anodic Tafel slopes (βc and βa ), the degree of surface coverage (θ) as well as the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) as a function of concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs are summarized in Table 2 . The θ and η values are calculated from the icorr values with the following equations [22,23]:
(1)
θ =
i
corr
0
-
i
corr
′
i
corr
0
(2)
η =
i
corr
0
-
i
corr
′
i
corr
0
× 100
where
i
corr
0
and
i
corr
′
are the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of the inhibitor, respectively.

From the visual examination of polarization curves in Fig. 7, both cathodic and anodic regions shift towards lower current density with the increase in the concentration of p-CDs and o-CDs, indicating that the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs has an effect on the cathodic and anodic reactions.

An inspection of the results in the Table 2 reveals that, βa value varies significantly in presence of p-CDs and o-CDs, which is due to the adsorption of the chloride ion or inhibitor molecules at the active sites of the metal surface. The variation of βc with the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs indicates the influence of the inhibitor on the kinetics of hydrogen evolution [24]. Also, with the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs to the 1 M HCl solution, the Ecorr value shows a slighter shift (less than 30 mV) towards a positive direction compared to the blank HCl, suggesting that the p-CDs and o-CDs are the mixed type inhibitors that can inhibit both the anodic dissolution of carbon steel and the cathodic reduction of H+ [20]. It is also evident that the icorr decreases, θ and η increases with increasing concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs owing to the absorption of the p-CDs and o-CDs on the metal surface. The lowest icorr value observed for the sample immersed in 1 M HCl solution in the presence of 200 mg/L p-CDs and o-CDs is 40.6 and 18.8 μA·cm−2, respectively. This result proves that p-CDs and o-CDs can act as effective corrosion inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution.

The corrosion process of the Q235 carbon steel in the corrosive medium can also be detected by the SVET method. The local current density (i) is obtained from the obtained potential signals in accord with Ohm’s law, as shown in the following equation
i = - Δ φ
k
d
( A
cm
- 2
)
, where Δφ is the electric potential drop, k is the electrolyte conductivity of the HCl solution (426.16 S m−1) and d is the vibration amplitude (30 μm) [25,26]. Fig. 8 presents the current density mapping of Q235 carbon steel after 2 h immersion in 1 M HCl solution with the absence and presence of 200 mg L−1 of p-CDs and o-CDs obtained via SVET method. For the Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution, the selected region of Q235 carbon steel exhibits an anodic zone with positive current density, which is associated with the anodic corrosion activity of the carbon steel (g. 8a). In contrast, the addition of p-CDs in 1 M HCl solution leads to a distinct reduction of anodic zone, indicating the suppression of the corrosion process at the surface of Q235 carbon steel (Fig. 8b). More interestingly, after the addition of the o-CDs (Fig. 8c), the anodic zone at the Q235 carbon steel disappears completely and the increase of cathodic current density can be observed at the upper of the Q235 carbon steel, confirming the absorption of the o-CDs at the surface of Q235 carbon steel can inhibit its corrosion.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was further performed to investigate the corrosion inhibition behavior of the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of p-CDs and o-CDs, as shown in Fig. 9 . For the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution, the Nyquist plot shows a large capacitive loop at high frequencies (HF) and a small inductive loop at low frequencies (LF). The HF capacitive loop is attributed to the charge transfer process at electrode/solution interface, and the LF inductive loop may be attributed to the relaxation process like Cl- ads and H+ ads on the metal substrate [27]. However, the shape of the Nyquist plots in the presence of p-CDs is different from that in blank HCl solution. The Nyquist plots only contain a depressed semicircle whose diameter increases with increasing concentrations of p-CDs (Fig. 9a). In case of the o-CDs (Fig. 9d), the shape and the diameter of the Nyquist plot for the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution with 10 mg·L−1 o-CDs is nearly same with that in blank HCl solution, indicating that there is no obvious corrosion inhibition in the presence of 10 mg/L o-CDs. But with increasing the concentrations of o-CDs, the shape of the Nyquist plots is similar with that in the presence of p-CDs and the diameter of the semicircle also increases with increasing concentrations of o-CDs. Besides, it is apparent from Bode plots that the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs results in an increase in the impedance modulus which further increases with increasing concentration of the corrosion inhibitors (Fig. 9b and e). This can be attributed to the formation of a protective film of the prepared p-CDs and o-CDs on steel surface which inhibits the dissolution of iron. As shown in Fig. 9c and f, the Bode-phase angle plots of the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of 10 mg/L o-CDs consist of a time constant for the corrosion process at the metal-solution interface and a portion of plot under the zero phase degree. In other cases, there is only a single time constant for the corrosion process at the metal-solution interface in the phase-angle plots (Fig. 9c and f). The increase in the peak heights with increasing concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs implies a more capacitive response at the metal-solution interface owing to the absorption of inhibitor molecules [24,28].

To better analyze the corrosion behavior of the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of p-CDs and o-CDs, electrical equivalent circuits in Fig. 10 are used to fit the EIS results and corresponding fitting parameters are listed in Table 3 . In the electrical equivalent circuits, Rs is the solution resistance, Rp corresponds to the polarisation resistance and CPEdl is a constant phase element used to instead of an ideal capacitor as a result of different physical phenomena like surface roughness, inhibitor adsorption, porous layer formation, etc. [23]. L is the inductance and RL is corresponding resistance. The appearance of inductance L in the EIS result implies that the dissolution of Q235 carbon steel is still going on through the direct charge transfer in the presence of the inhibitor [29]. The data in the Table 3 clearly shows that Rp value increases while CPEdl tends to decrease with increasing concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs in 1 M HCl solution. The increase in Rp value and the decrease of CPEdl is attributed to the formation of absorption film at the metal/solution interface. Absorbed inhibitor film at the steel/solution interface can replace water molecules by inhibitor molecules or increase the double layer thickness, thus leading to the reduction of CPEdl values. The largest inhibiting effect is observed at 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs, which gives Rp value of 547 and 759 Ω cm2, respectively.

To determine the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency of the Q235 carbon steel in the 1 M HCl solutions with various concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs, the Q235 carbon steel substrates are immersed in the test solutions and weighed after 2 h immersion. The corresponding corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency are calculated according to the following equations [30,31]:
(3)
Corrosion rate =
Δ
m
mean
At
(4)
Inhibition efficiency =
W
corr
-
W
corr
′
W
corr
× 100
where
W
corr
is the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution and
W
corr
′
corresponds to the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution with various concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs.
Δ
m
mean
is the average weight loss of the Q235 carbon steel for three experiments. A is the surface area of the Q235 carbon steel (1 cm2) and t is the immersion time.

Fig. 11 a and b presents the variation of corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency of the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution with the absence and presence of various concentrations of p-CDs and o-CDs. It can be observed that the corrosion rate of the Q235 carbon steel decreases with the increase in the concentration of p-PDs and o-CDs while the inhibition efficiency shows a crosscurrent. The inhibition efficiency is close to 90% after 2 h immersion for p-CDs and o-CDs.

The experimental data obtained from weight loss measurements are found to fit well with “Langmuir” adsorption isotherms. The adsorption isotherms are deduced by fitting the C/θ as a function of the concentration of p-CDs and o-CDs according to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm (Eqs. (5) and (6)) [32,33], as shown in Fig. 11c and d.
(5)
θ =
W
corr
-
W
corr
′
W
corr
(6)
C
inh
θ
=
1
K
ads
+
C
inh
where θ is the surface coverage,
C
inh
is the concentration of corrosion inhibitor and
K
ads
is the adsorption equilibrium constant.

In Fig. 11c and d, a good linear correlation between C/θ and C is observed for p-CDs and o-CDs (with an R2 of 0.9999 and 0.9994 for p-CDs and o-CDs, respectively), indicating that the adsorption process obeys the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The Kads value for p-CDs and o-CDs is 657.9 and 75.5 L/g for p-CDs and o-CDs, respectively. The high Kads value indicates the strong adsorption of inhibitor molecules on steel surface in HCl solution. Thus, the adsorption free energy
Δ
G
ads
0
value of the CDs can be determined using the following equation [34,35]:
(7)
Δ
G
ads
0
= - RT ln ( 1000
K
ads
)
where R is the molar gas constant and T is the absolute temperature (298 K). In the above equation 1000 g/L is used for concentration of water, rather than 55.5 mol/L because unit of Kads has been reported in terms of L·g−1 [24].

Usually, the
Δ
G
ads
0
values are used to evaluate the adsorption type of corrosion inhibitor [28,36]. While
Δ
G
ads
0
value is −20 kJ·mol−1 or more positive, there is an electrostatic interaction between inhibitor molecules and metal surface, which is called as physical adsorption. While the
Δ
G
ads
0
value is −40 kJ·mol−1 or more negative, transfer or sharing of electrons occurs from inhibitor molecules to metal surface and thus coordinate covalent bond is formed, which is named as chemical adsorption. The
Δ
G
ads
0
value is −33.19 and −27.83 kJ·mol−1 for the p-CDs and o-CDs, respectively, indicating that the interaction between CDs (p-CDs and o-CDs) and Q235 carbon steel surface involves both chemisorption and physisorption.

In general, the heteroatoms in inhibitor molecules like nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygen can be regarded as the reaction center to the adsorption process. In addition, the steric effect and electric charge of the inhibitor molecules also has a significant influence on the adsorption and hence the effectiveness of the inhibitor [Corrosion Inhibition of N80 Steel Using Novel Diquaternary Ammonium Salts in 15% Hydrochloric Acid]. For p-CDs and o-CDs, they not only contain two nitrogen and oxygen atoms but also have charge transfer character. As confirmed by UV–vis absorption of p-CDs and o-CDs, there are three characteristic peaks assigned to π–π∗ transitions, n–π∗ transitions and the excitonic transition in quinoid-imine units of long conjugated polymer chain. These features are favorable to facilitate the adsorption of inhibitor on the metal surface and the improvement of inhibition efficiency.
The effect of immersion time on the corrosion behavior of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs is also investigated.

Fig. 12 shows the variation of the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency in the absence and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs after various immersion times at room temperature. Results reveal the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs inhibits the corrosion of the Q235 carbon steel significantly. The corrosion rate of the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution decreases with increasing immersion time, which is about 1.8 × 10−3 g cm−2 h−1 after 72 h immersion. For the Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution in the presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs, the corrosion rate also decreases gradually with prolonged immersion time, which is 5.2 × 10−5 and 1.8 × 10−5 g cm−2 h−1, respectively. Inhibition efficiency in the presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs shows a slight increase with increasing immersion time, which reaches about 97% and 99%, respectively, after 72 h immersion (Fig. 12b). These results indicate that the p-CDs and o-CDs can act as effective corrosion inhibitor for the Q235 carbon steel during the long-term immersion process.

After 72 h immersion, the surface roughness (Ra ) of the Q235 carbon steel is determined by LSCM, as given in Fig. 13 . Ra value of the polished Q235 carbon steel before immersion is about 0.62 μm (Fig. 13a). After 72 h immersion in 1 M HCl solution, Ra value of the sample is about 3.52 μm (Fig. 13b). In case of the presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs, Ra value decreases dramatically with respect to that in 1 M HCl solution, which is 1.00 μm and 1.18 μm, respectively (Fig. 13c and d). Results suggest that the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs can retard the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution in some extent while corrosion still exists in the surface of the samples.

SEM images of the Q235 carbon steel before and after 72 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs and o-CDs are also obtained, as shown in Fig. 14 . It can be observed from Fig. 14a that the polished Q235 carbon steel is relative smooth with some polished scratches. In case of the Q235 carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution (Fig. 14b), the surface is very rough and has some cracks, pits and flakes owing to the severe corrosion damage. However, in the presence of 200 mg/L of p-CDs (Fig. 14c) and o-CDs (Fig. 14d), the corrosion of the Q235 carbon steel is inhibited significantly and there is less pitting in the Q235 carbon steel surface with respect to that in 1 M HCl solution. This observation clearly proves that the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs suppresses the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel significantly via forming a protective film.

The EDS spectra and corresponding percentage weight contents are displayed in Fig. 15 a–d and Table 4 . Fig. 15a is the EDS spectrum of the polished Q235 carbon steel, which shows the characteristic element peaks of C (6.7 wt%), O (1.4 wt%) and Fe (91.9 wt%). For the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution, the EDS spectrum and the content of the elements vary dramatically. The intensity of Fe peak decreases owing to the dissolution of Fe (57.6 wt%) and other peaks for minor elements (Cr, Cu, As, Al, P, Si, Ni) become obvious (6.8 wt%). Also, the intensity of oxygen (16.7 wt%) increases owing to the oxidation of the Q235 carbon steel surface. However, in the presence of p-CDs and o-CDs, the EDS spectra are different with that of polished Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. The decrease in the content of Fe in the presence of p-CDs and o-CDs is slighter than the sample in 1 M HCl solution, which is 79.3 wt% and 83.7 wt%, respectively. These data also indicate that the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel is retarded significantly by the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs.

Eventually, the difference of corrosion inhibition between p-CDs and o-CDs is compared according to the above results. It can be found from electrochemical results that corrosion inhibition of p-CDs and o-CDs increases with increasing concentrations. When the concentration is 200 mg/L, the corrosion inhibition of p-CDs has little variation than that in 100 mg/L of p-CDs while the corrosion inhibition of o-CDs exhibits better corrosion inhibition than p-CDs. Further, during the long time immersion, o-CDs have lower corrosion rate and higher corrosion inhibition efficiency than p-CDs.

The corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution could be prevented by the addition of p-CDs and o-CDs. And these two NCDs showed a high inhibitive efficiency for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. The principal conclusions are emphasized as follows:
(1)
Based on the results of polarization measurements, SVET and EIS, p-CDs and o-CDs could be seen as effective inhibitor for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. And the inhibition efficiency was in direct proportion with the concentration of the p-CDs and o-CDs.
(2)
According to the data extracted from the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the absorption of p-CDs and o-CDs involved both chemisorption and physisorption.
(3)
Weight loss measurements indicated that inhibition efficiency was greater than 97% and varied slightly with increasing immersion time. And o-CDs have better corrosion inhibition efficiency than p-CDs
(4)
The LSCM, SEM and EDS results revealed that less damage was observed at the surface of Q235 carbon steel in the presence of p-CDs and o-CDs, demonstrating the effective role of p-CDs and o-CDs in corrosion inhibition of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution.

The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by the Open Financial Grant from Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology (QNLM2016ORP0409), the Key Research Projects of Frontier Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences (QYZDY-SSW-JSC009) and “One Hundred Talented People” of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Y60707WR04).
